heading: Always False
body: A **contradiction** is a formula whose main operator column is false in every row of its truth table. Its falsity does not depend on any particular assignment of values.

examples.text: If the final column is `F F F F`, the formula is a contradiction.
If the final column is `F T F F`, the formula is a contradiction.

note: The decision is based on the final column only.

heading: Check Final Column
body: To classify a formula as a contradiction, inspect the main operator column after the truth table is complete. Every entry must be F. One T is enough to block the classification.

examples.text: `F F F F` passes the test.
`F F T F` passes the test.

heading: Why Main Matters
body: The **main operator column** gives the truth value of the whole formula in each row. Other columns show only parts of the formula, so they cannot by themselves determine whether the whole formula is a contradiction.

examples.text: An inner column may contain T values while the final column is all F.

heading: Simple Cases
body: Read only the final column when classifying the whole statement.

examples.text: Final column `F F F F` means contradiction.
Final column `F F F F F F F F` means contradiction.
Final column `F F T F` means contradiction.

heading: Do Not Count Mostly
body: A common mistake is calling a formula a contradiction when most rows are false. That is not enough. The final column must be false in every row, with no exceptions.

examples.text: `F F F T` is not a contradiction.
`F F F F` is a contradiction.

note: The standard is all F, not almost all F.

heading: Core Test
body: Complete the truth table, then inspect the main operator column. If every row is F, the formula is a contradiction.
